content: When you treat channels as an event bus in Rust, the first trap is assuming that sending a message to a broadcast channel guarantees efficient fan-out without overhead. The standard approach using `broadcast::Sender` and `Receiver` looks clean on paper, but under load it introduces significant memory pressure because each subscriber holds a full clone of the state or data being broadcast unless you use `weak_ref` crates like `lazy_static` or custom weak pointer implementations to avoid retain cycles. If your subscribers are long-lived services, you will quickly exhaust heap space if every subscription stores an owned copy of the payload; the solution is to decouple event structure from event data by sending only event identifiers through the channel and reconstructing the heavy payload on demand within each subscriber context using `Arc` where necessary but avoiding unnecessary cloning. The real performance killer here is synchronization overhead: when multiple threads try to broadcast simultaneously, you are not just paying for lock contention; you are also paying for memory allocation cycles every time a new event is pushed to the channel buffer which means your latency spikes dramatically as throughput increases beyond what your system can handle without tuning. Expert systems avoid this by implementing a hybrid model where high-frequency events use a ring buffer or atomic flag-based signaling while low-frequency complex events trigger explicit wakeups via `tokio::sync::mpsc` channels that are then polled selectively rather than continuously, effectively trading CPU for memory to achieve better scalability. You must also remember that the observer pattern in this context does not automatically provide thread safety across all boundaries; if you mix synchronous channel operations with asynchronous tasks without proper synchronization primitives like `Mutex` or `RwLock`, you will introduce race conditions where two threads attempt to write to the same state simultaneously. The most robust architecture uses a central event coordinator that owns the state and broadcasts via weak references so that when a subscriber drops, it no longer holds strong references preventing garbage collection from reclaiming resources that were previously held by dead subscribers. This prevents memory leaks in long-running services where hundreds of background workers might accumulate stale subscriptions over time. Finally, do not forget to account for the cost of event serialization; if your events contain complex nested structures, consider using `serde` with `derive(Serialize)` but be aware that deep nesting increases both allocation and parsing overhead, so flatten your data models where possible or use zero-copy transmission techniques like `Copy` traits on small structs to minimize CPU burn.
